label: delphi-r1-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the advice a cautious public-agency counsel would give, then ask whether caution is legally required or merely prudent.
text: {"probability":80,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"A court would very likely hold both halves of the proposition true: (1) the Signal messages were \"public records\" under San Jose's content-based test because they concerned franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will realistically accept,\" and RFP timing — core public-business content indistinguishable from the personal-device texts/emails San Jose held were public records regardless of storage location; and (2) the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer, issued after searching only city systems and never asking any councilmember to search personal devices/accounts, fell short of the reasonable-diligence search standard the CPRA requires — especially since the request explicitly sought records \"on any platform or account\" and at least 40 extant screenshots were never located or produced. The compound proposition is not a close call on either prong individually, but real uncertainty remains on the precise legal contours of the \"duty to inquire\" (as distinct from the duty to disclose records once found), which is less definitively fixed by binding appellate authority than the public-record-content question, and on how a court treats the fact that most messages were already gone before the request arrived.","rationales":["San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes content, not custody or device location, the touchstone for 'public record' status under Gov. Code § 6252(e), and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing is core public-business content that maps almost exactly onto San Jose's own fact pattern of personal-device texts/emails about city business.","The CPRA request expressly sought councilmember communications 'on any platform or account,' which put the clerk on direct notice that personal-account records were within scope, so a search confined to city systems is facially inadequate under the reasonable-diligence standard the Act requires for locating responsive records.","At least 40 screenshots indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at (and presumably after) the time of the response, so independent of the disputed status of the auto-deleted messages, the city demonstrably failed to locate and produce records that did exist, which is a comparatively clean basis for a violation finding.","San Jose remanded without fully resolving search-methodology standards but signaled that agencies may need to reach out to individual officials/employees regarding personal accounts, and post-2017 municipal best-practice guidance (e.g., League of California Cities model policies) treats 'ask the record-holder to search their own device' as baseline compliance, making a total omission of that step an easier case for a court to fault.","The involvement of three of five councilmembers in sustained substantive policy discussion (diversion targets, what council will accept) independently corroborates that the content was governmental deliberation rather than personal chatter, reinforcing the public-record characterization and undercutting any argument the messages were merely informal or preliminary in a way that would exempt them."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 6252(e) (definition of 'public records')","Gov. Code § 6253(a)-(c) (duty to make records promptly available; reasonable diligence in responding)","Gov. Code § 6253.1 (agency's duty to assist requester, including communicating with staff/officials likely to have responsive records)","Gov. Code § 34090 (retention/destruction schedule for local agency records)","Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (search-scope/duty-to-search issues, cited cautiously as a possible analog — verify applicability, since it concerns third-party vendor records rather than personal-account communications of officials)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will treat the near-total destruction of the underlying messages (all but 40 screenshots) as narrowing the \"violation\" finding to only the located screenshots — i.e., framing the bulk of the dispute as a retention/spoliation problem separate from CPRA search-adequacy — versus treating the clerk's total failure to even ask councilmembers about personal accounts as itself an independent, freestanding CPRA violation regardless of what a proper search would have ultimately recovered; there is no squarely on-point binding appellate decision fixing the precise contours of the \"duty to inquire\" of individual officials post-San Jose."}

data.position_summary: A court would very likely hold both halves of the proposition true: (1) the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test because they concerned franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and RFP timing — core public-business content indistinguishable from the personal-device texts/emails San Jose held were public records regardless of storage location; and (2) the clerk's "no responsive records" answer, issued after searching only city systems and never asking any councilmember to search personal devices/accounts, fell short of the reasonable-diligence search standard the CPRA requires — especially since the request explicitly sought records "on any platform or account" and at least 40 extant screenshots were never located or produced. The compound proposition is not a close call on either prong individually, but real uncertainty remains on the precise legal contours of the "duty to inquire" (as distinct from the duty to disclose records once found), which is less definitively fixed by binding appellate authority than the public-record-content question, and on how a court treats the fact that most messages were already gone before the request arrived.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes content, not custody or device location, the touchstone for 'public record' status under Gov. Code § 6252(e), and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing is core public-business content that maps almost exactly onto San Jose's own fact pattern of personal-device texts/emails about city business.

The CPRA request expressly sought councilmember communications 'on any platform or account,' which put the clerk on direct notice that personal-account records were within scope, so a search confined to city systems is facially inadequate under the reasonable-diligence standard the Act requires for locating responsive records.

At least 40 screenshots indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at (and presumably after) the time of the response, so independent of the disputed status of the auto-deleted messages, the city demonstrably failed to locate and produce records that did exist, which is a comparatively clean basis for a violation finding.

San Jose remanded without fully resolving search-methodology standards but signaled that agencies may need to reach out to individual officials/employees regarding personal accounts, and post-2017 municipal best-practice guidance (e.g., League of California Cities model policies) treats 'ask the record-holder to search their own device' as baseline compliance, making a total omission of that step an easier case for a court to fault.

The involvement of three of five councilmembers in sustained substantive policy discussion (diversion targets, what council will accept) independently corroborates that the content was governmental deliberation rather than personal chatter, reinforcing the public-record characterization and undercutting any argument the messages were merely informal or preliminary in a way that would exempt them.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 6253(a)-(c) (duty to make records promptly available; reasonable diligence in responding)
Gov. Code § 6253.1 (agency's duty to assist requester, including communicating with staff/officials likely to have responsive records)
Gov. Code § 34090 (retention/destruction schedule for local agency records)

Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (search-scope/duty-to-search issues, cited cautiously as a possible analog — verify applicability, since it concerns third-party vendor records rather than personal-account communications of officials)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat the near-total destruction of the underlying messages (all but 40 screenshots) as narrowing the "violation" finding to only the located screenshots — i.e., framing the bulk of the dispute as a retention/spoliation problem separate from CPRA search-adequacy — versus treating the clerk's total failure to even ask councilmembers about personal accounts as itself an independent, freestanding CPRA violation regardless of what a proper search would have ultimately recovered; there is no squarely on-point binding appellate decision fixing the precise contours of the "duty to inquire" of individual officials post-San Jose.
